classification: Open-source memory forensics framework used in incident response and malware analysis
short_description: Volatility is a Python-based framework for extracting and analyzing digital artifacts from volatile memory snapshots across Windows, Linux, and macOS; Volatility 3 is a modern rewrite that uses symbol tables instead of static profiles and a layered architecture to parse diverse memory image formats.
overview_notes: The Volatility Foundation maintains Volatility as a widely used platform for above-OS memory analysis in DFIR and malware workflows. In 2019 the Foundation released Volatility 3, a ground-up rewrite addressing architectural and performance limitations of Volatility 2 and introducing a new licensing model (Volatility Software License). Volatility 3 aims to fully replace Volatility 2 for modern investigations, with ongoing releases and a growing community plugin ecosystem.
what_it_is_notes:
- A cross-platform, extensible framework for post-acquisition analysis of RAM images that reveals runtime system state independent of the compromised system.
- Implements a plugin model for common forensic tasks (process and thread enumeration, module/DLL listing, handle and registry inspection, network artifact parsing, keyword/indicator scanning, object recovery, etc.).
- Volatility 3 replaces Volatility 2’s OS-specific profiles with symbol tables (Intermediate Symbol Files, ISF) and formalizes a context/layer/object abstraction for image parsing.
how_it_works_notes:
- Layered architecture: raw image data and format-specific layers (e.g., AVML, LiME, Windows crash/hibernation) stack under CPU/MMU translation layers (e.g., Intel) to reconstruct virtual/physical address spaces within a Context container. Plugins request reads through these layers, which transparently translate addresses and combine sources when needed.
- Symbols and object model: ISF symbol tables provide type information for kernel/user structures; objects are materialized from symbols to traverse OS internals. Windows symbols can be fetched/generated automatically; Linux/macOS ISFs are typically generated with dwarf2json.
- Plugins operate against reconstructed memory views and symbolized structures to extract artifacts. Community plugins can be added without modifying core.
- Analysis is host-agnostic: extraction occurs offline from the memory image, not by executing code on the suspect system.
inputs: Volatile memory images from Windows, Linux, and macOS systems
Supported formats via layers (examples include raw/padded dumps, Windows crash dumps, Windows hibernation, LiME, AVML, certain hypervisor/VM formats as available through corresponding layers)
Associated symbol packs or ISFs (Windows, macOS, Linux)
outputs: Structured listings of processes/threads and parent-child trees
Loaded kernel modules, drivers, and user-mode modules/DLLs
Network-related artifacts where supported (e.g., connection tables on specific Windows versions)
Registry and configuration artifacts (where available in memory)
YARA-based hit reports over memory regions
Recovered data structures and selected dumps of memory regions/objects for offline examination
core_concepts: Plugin-based extensibility for artifacts and OS support
Independence from live system state (resistant to rootkit hiding in live tools)
typical_workflow: Acquire memory from target system using a trusted acquisition tool, preserving chain of custody.
Prepare or obtain matching symbol tables (Windows automatic fetch/generation; Linux/macOS via dwarf2json or curated packs).
Load image in Volatility 3; framework auto-stacks relevant layers for the format.
Run foundational discovery plugins (system/version info, process/module enumeration) to validate parsing and scope analysis.
Pivot to targeted plugins (e.g., process scans, injected-code heuristics, registry- or credential-relevant artifacts, YARA scans).
Correlate with other evidence sources (disk, telemetry, network) and export selected objects/regions for deeper analysis.
Document findings with timestamps, offsets, and tool/version metadata.
use_cases: Malware and rootkit investigations where in-memory state reveals injected code, hollowed processes, hooks, or hidden objects.
Rapid incident response triage to enumerate processes, drivers, and connections without trusting the live OS.
Ransomware investigations to identify responsible processes, keys present in memory, or runtime configuration artifacts.
Credential artifact hunting (e.g., LSASS-related memory regions) subject to legal/ethical constraints.
Detection and study of fileless or LOLBin-based techniques that primarily reside in memory.
Validation and research of boot- or kernel-level tampering as reflected in runtime structures (with deeper boot/firmware analysis requiring dedicated tools).
limitations: Symbol dependency: Accurate parsing requires correct symbol tables; Windows symbols are often automated, but Linux/macOS usually need custom ISFs via dwarf2json, and kernel variance can impede analysis.
Coverage variations: Some plugins target specific OS versions or have limited functionality depending on kernel changes; parity with Volatility 2 has improved but not every legacy behavior maps 1:1.
Image-format constraints: Certain hibernation/crash/VM formats and very new OS builds may need updated layers or symbols; unsupported formats require conversion or alternative acquisition.
Anti-forensics and noise: Memory compression, paging, and deliberate tampering can obscure artifacts; scans may yield false positives/negatives.
Operational boundaries: Volatility analyzes acquired memory; it does not perform acquisition itself, and legal/privacy considerations govern what can be extracted or reviewed.
Performance and resource use: Large images and symbol caching can be time- and memory-intensive, especially on first runs or with broad scans.

related_tools.name: WinPmem
related_tools.relationship: Memory acquisition for Windows; produces images Volatility can analyze.

related_tools.name: AVML
related_tools.relationship: Microsoft’s acquisition tool for Linux; Volatility 3 has an AVML layer for parsing resulting images.

related_tools.name: LiME
related_tools.relationship: Linux Memory Extractor; Volatility supports LiME-formatted images via a dedicated layer.

related_tools.name: DumpIt
related_tools.relationship: Windows memory acquisition; outputs compatible raw images.

related_tools.name: dwarf2json
related_tools.relationship: Volatility Foundation tool to generate Linux/macOS ISF symbol tables consumed by Volatility 3.

related_tools.name: MemProcFS
related_tools.relationship: Alternative memory forensics/analysis environment; often used alongside or as a complement to Volatility.

related_tools.name: Rekall
related_tools.relationship: Alternative/open-source memory forensics framework historically related to Volatility 2 lineage.

related_tools.name: Sysmon
related_tools.relationship: Host telemetry source; Volatility findings are often correlated with Sysmon/Event Logs.

related_tools.name: YARA
related_tools.relationship: Pattern-matching engine leveraged by Volatility’s yarascan plugin for indicator hunting.

related_tools.name: Cuckoo Sandbox
related_tools.relationship: Dynamic malware analysis environment; memory dumps from sandbox VMs can be analyzed with Volatility.

evidence_gaps: A fully authoritative, consolidated matrix of all image formats and OS build/version support in Volatility 3 is dispersed across code/doc modules; practitioners should verify format and plugin support per release.
Public documentation confirms Volatility 3 ‘parity’ with Volatility 2 at a high level, but precise feature-by-feature parity status and deprecations vary across versions and plugins.
UEFI/bootkit-specific analysis is not a primary focus of Volatility and may require specialized tools; Volatility’s role is indirect via kernel/runtime artifacts.

sources.title: Volatility Foundation – Official parity release announcement for Volatility 3
